chunks.text: In every region of the world
there is a ritual or a festival unique to that land.
They reflect the people's view of the world, their sense of religion
and their relationship with nature.
In Japan, the first shrine visit of the year, Setsubun, Obon
and the festivals of each region are still carefully kept.
The Gion Festival of Kyoto has a history of more than a thousand years
and the Nebuta Festival of Aomori fills the night streets with passion.
Such tradition is not merely a habit of the past.
It is also a place where the people of a community confirm their own identity — who they are.
In the world, there are rite of passage that celebrate birth, adulthood, marriage, and death.
These are events in which the whole community stands beside a person at a turning point of life and sends them on toward a new stage.
But the wave of globalization is making the unique cultures of each place uniform.
Young people move to the cities, and the bearers of local festivals continue to decline.
Many traditional crafts have already disappeared.
On the other hand, there is also a movement to preserve such fading cultures.
UNESCO registers the traditional cultures of various regions of the world as intangible heritage.
Japanese food, Noh theater, and the making of Japanese paper are among these.
Tradition is not a relic of the past.
It is a living gift that we, the people who live now
hand down to the next generation.
